title: Sandbox with Fold-Out Seats
summary: A 4-foot sandbox whose lid folds open into two bench seats with backrests — closed, it keeps cats and rain out; open, the kids sit on it instead of in the mud.
description:
The clever part of this sandbox is that the lid is the furniture. Two hinged plank panels cover the box completely when it's closed — keeping out rain, leaves, and every neighborhood cat, which is not optional for a sandbox — and each panel folds open in two stages: the first fold lays flat as a seat, the second folds up into a backrest, held by the 2x4 armrests. Kids sit ON the box edge with back support, and the sand stays where it belongs.

The geometry has one rule that makes or breaks the folding: consistent 1/2" gaps between planks, and the 2x4 seat supports inset at least 1" from the panel edges. The gaps are the hinge clearance — planks butted tight will bind the fold — and the inset lets the backrest sections nest into the box when closed. Dry-fit the folding action before final screws.

Use exterior hinges (they're the moving part; cheap ones seize in a season) and check the folds for finger-pinch points with the same paranoia you'd use on a toy box. Set the box on landscape fabric, not bare grass, before the sand goes in — it drains while keeping the weeds down.

tools.note: Faster for the batch of identical 1x4 crosscuts.
Pre-drill and countersink every screw through the top planks — kids' legs slide across these.
One option for the box corners; pre-drilled screws through the sides work too.
Every edge, aggressively. Bare legs and small hands live on this thing.

materials.name: Exterior hinges
materials.note: Four sets. The moving part of the whole design — buy exterior-rated, not the cheapest card.

materials.name: Exterior gate handles or rope
materials.note: For lifting the folds. 'Gate pull' is the search term.

materials.name: Landscape fabric
materials.note: Under the box — drains rain, blocks weeds. Sand not included in the estimate.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body:
Join the sides into a square, short sides inset between the long sides. Pocket holes or pre-drilled 1-1/4" screws through the long sides — either is fine on a box this size.

Check the diagonals match before moving on; the folding panels are rectangles, and they only close cleanly over a rectangular box.

steps.title: Fix the outer planks
steps.body: Screw two planks to the box top on each side — these are the only planks that don't move. Countersink the screws through the top and keep the 1/2" gap between planks; that spacing continues across the whole folding surface.

steps.title: Build the two seat panels
steps.body:
Screw two planks (1/2" apart) across each pair of 2x4 armrests. Inset the 2x4s AT LEAST 1" from the plank ends — that inset is what lets the assembly fold down into the box without jamming.

Build two.

steps.title: Hinge the seats to the fixed planks
steps.body: Attach the first set of hinges to each seat panel on the side OPPOSITE the armrests, then attach that hinged edge to the fixed planks on the box. (Easier in that order — the source builder learned it the fumbling way.) Keep the 1/2" gap at the hinge line; it's the clearance the fold pivots through.

steps.title: Build the two backrest panels
steps.body: Same construction as the seats: two planks, 1/2" apart, screwed across the 12-1/2" back support 2x4s. The longer 2x4s do double duty — they're the backrest structure when open and the lift handles when closed.

steps.title: Hinge the backrests and test the fold
steps.body:
Attach the second set of hinges to each backrest on the side opposite its 2x4s, then hinge it to the seat panel — easiest with the seat folded up. Same 1/2" gap.

Now cycle it: closed lid → fold once to seat → fold again to backrest, both sides. It should move without force. Add the gate handles, sand every edge and corner like it's a toy (it is), check the folds for pinch points, and fill with sand — on landscape fabric, so the box drains.

images.alt: Wooden sandbox with lid panels folded open into two bench seats with backrests
